target.title: test(cli): cover node daemon lifecycle helpers
target.body:
## Summary
- Add focused tests for the node daemon CLI helper surface.
- Cover the already-installed `node install --json` short-circuit so it does not rebuild or reinstall the service.
- Cover `node status --json` payload construction from the resolved node service.

Refs #83924

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: the node daemon lifecycle helper file had no direct tests for service install/status behavior. This left paths such as the already-installed install short-circuit and JSON status payload construction uncovered.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, Node.js v22.22.0, branch `test/node-cli-daemon-lifecycle-83924`, using the real `src/cli/node-cli/daemon.ts` implementation with mocked service/config boundaries under the repo's Vitest wrapper.

Exact steps or command run after fix:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/node-cli/daemon.test.ts src/cli/node-cli/register.test.ts
$ git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix: Targeted test output:

```console
✓ cli ../../src/cli/node-cli/daemon.test.ts (2 tests) 7ms
✓ cli ../../src/cli/node-cli/register.test.ts (1 test) 2ms

Test Files 2 passed (2)
Tests 3 passed (3)
```

Observed result after fix: The new daemon tests verify `runNodeDaemonInstall({ json: true })` skips install planning and service installation when the node service is already loaded, and verify `runNodeDaemonStatus({ json: true })` emits a JSON payload containing loaded service metadata, command details, and runtime status.

What was not tested: I did not exercise real launchd/systemd/schtasks commands; the tests intentionally mock the service boundary to cover CLI decision logic without installing or managing a real service.

## Test plan
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/node-cli/daemon.test.ts src/cli/node-cli/register.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(agents): inherit subagent thinking defaults
target.body:
## Summary

- Subagent spawns now inherit the requester session's persisted thinking level when the spawn request and agent config do not specify one.
- The fallback order is explicit spawn thinking, requester agent subagent default, target agent subagent default, global subagent default, then requester session thinking.
- Existing explicit/configured thinking behavior is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #55790
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

A user-selected session thinking level should carry into subagents by default. Before this change, a parent session could run with one thinking setting while a child session silently lost that setting unless the spawn request or config repeated it.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `sessions_spawn(runtime="subagent")` now preserves requester session thinking as the final fallback when no explicit or configured subagent thinking is set.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout in a Linux container, on branch `fix-subagent-thinking-defaults`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts`
  - `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn-applies-thinking-default.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts`
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured from the patched branch:
  ```text
  $ git branch --show-current
  fix-subagent-thinking-defaults

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts
  Test Files  1 passed
  Tests       10 passed

  $ ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn-applies-thinking-default.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts
  Test Files  1 passed
  Tests       5 passed
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The spawn-path coverage persists the inherited requester `thinkingLevel` onto the child session, while explicit/configured thinking continues to take precedence.
- What was not tested: A live provider-backed subagent run.


## Root Cause (if applicable)

The subagent thinking resolver only knew about explicit spawn input and configured subagent defaults. It did not receive requester session thinking, so it had no way to preserve the caller's persisted thinking level.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn-applies-thinking-default.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts`
- Locked-in scenario: caller session thinking is inherited only after explicit and configured subagent thinking options are absent.
- Existing coverage before this patch covered explicit and configured defaults, but not caller-session inheritance.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Subagents spawned without explicit or configured subagent thinking now inherit the requester session's thinking level.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
explicit spawn thinking
  -> requester agent subagent thinking
  -> target agent subagent thinking
  -> global subagent thinking
  -> requester session thinking
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- No security-impacting changes identified.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux container
- Runtime/container: Local OpenClaw checkout
- Model/provider: N/A; tests use mocked spawn/runtime paths
- Integration/channel: N/A
- Relevant config: test config with `agents.defaults.subagents.thinking` and mocked requester session store entries

### Steps

1. Run the focused resolver test.
2. Run the focused subagent spawn test.
3. Confirm explicit/configured thinking still wins.
4. Confirm requester thinking is persisted to the child session only when no higher-precedence thinking is set.

### Expected

Subagent thinking precedence is explicit spawn value, requester agent default, target agent default, global default, then requester session thinking.

### Actual

The new tests match that precedence and verify child-session persistence.

## Evidence

Included evidence:

- Focused test commands and passing results are listed above under real behavior proof.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: resolver precedence and child-session persistence.
- Edge cases checked: explicit thinking still overrides defaults; global default still overrides requester thinking.
- Not verified: live provider-backed subagent execution.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a child session may now use a higher thinking effort when the parent session is configured that way.
- Mitigation: explicit spawn thinking and configured subagent defaults still take precedence.

## Pi real behavior proof — 2026-05-20

Behavior or issue addressed:
- Verifies native sessions_spawn(runtime="subagent") thinking-default inheritance on the PR branch, including persisted child-session thinkingLevel behavior.

Real environment tested:
- Real Raspberry Pi node: pi-claw.
- Architecture/kernel: aarch64, Linux 6.18.29+rpt-rpi-2712.
- Branch checkout on Pi: stevenepalmer:fix-subagent-thinking-defaults.
- Commit tested: 558cbe261a.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
~~~text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs   src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts   src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn-applies-thinking-default.test.ts
~~~

Evidence after fix:
~~~text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts
src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn-applies-thinking-default.test.ts
  6 tests passed

[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts
src/agents/subagent-spawn.test.ts
  11 tests passed

[test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 24.92s
~~~

Observed result after fix:
- The Pi branch checkout passed the sessions_spawn thinking-default and persisted child-session thinkingLevel coverage.
- Tested precedence: explicit spawn -> requester agent config -> target agent config -> global config -> requester session thinking.

What was not tested:
- No paid external model provider call was made; the proof focuses on the native spawn planning/session persistence behavior that controls the thinkingLevel default.

## Live native sessions_spawn proof — 2026-05-20

Behavior or issue addressed:
- Adds the rank-up proof ClawSweeper requested: a live native sessions_spawn(runtime="subagent") run with omitted child thinking, showing the child inherited the parent thinking level.

Real environment tested:
- Running OpenClaw gateway: 2026.5.18.
- Parent session: agent:main:dashboard:c0098499-aab4-4abe-a285-e866e299c320.
- Child session: agent:main:subagent:6ba2b844-766e-40a5-9a9e-808364c22bef.
- Run id: 9592e938-8b19-4309-84cf-46bb60227c85.
- Auth/account values are redacted below.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
~~~text
1. Read parent session status.
2. Call native sessions_spawn with runtime="subagent" and no thinking field:
   taskName: pr_84007_live_thinking_proof
   task: Live proof for PR #84007. Reply exactly: PR_84007_CHILD_DONE
   cleanup: keep
   context: isolated
3. Read child session status after completion.
4. Inspect child runtime trajectory for session.started/model.completed evidence.
~~~

Evidence after fix:
~~~text
Parent session status:
OpenClaw 2026.5.18
Model: openai-codex/gpt-5.5
Session: agent:main:dashboard:c0098499-aab4-4abe-a285-e866e299c320
Execution: direct · Runtime: OpenAI Codex · Think: medium · Text: low

sessions_spawn input:
{
  "runtime": "subagent",
  "mode": "run",
  "taskName": "pr_84007_live_thinking_proof",
  "cleanup": "keep",
  "context": "isolated",
  "task": "Live proof for PR #84007. Reply exactly: PR_84007_CHILD_DONE"
}

sessions_spawn result:
{
  "status": "accepted",
  "childSessionKey": "agent:main:subagent:6ba2b844-766e-40a5-9a9e-808364c22bef",
  "runId": "9592e938-8b19-4309-84cf-46bb60227c85",
  "mode": "run",
  "taskName": "pr_84007_live_thinking_proof",
  "modelApplied": true
}

Child session status:
OpenClaw 2026.5.18
Model: openai/gpt-5.5
Session: agent:main:subagent:6ba2b844-766e-40a5-9a9e-808364c22bef
Tasks: latest succeeded · subagent · Live proof for PR #84007. Reply exactly: PR_84007_CHILD_DONE
Execution: direct · Runtime: OpenAI Codex · Think: medium · Text: low

Child trajectory excerpts:
session.started:
  sessionKey=agent:main:subagent:6ba2b844-766e-40a5-9a9e-808364c22bef
  runId=9592e938-8b19-4309-84cf-46bb60227c85
  provider=openai-codex
  modelId=gpt-5.5
  authProfileId=[REDACTED]

model.completed:
  assistantTexts=["PR_84007_CHILD_DONE"]
  timedOut=false
  promptError=null
~~~

Observed result after fix:
- The parent live session was running with Think: medium.
- The native sessions_spawn call omitted the thinking field.
- The created child subagent session also ran with Think: medium and completed the assigned task.
- This shows the child inherited the parent thinking level for a live native subagent spawn.

What was not tested:
- I did not include private auth profile values or full prompts in this proof; those were redacted.
- Separate Raspberry Pi branch-checkout proof remains above for the PR branch tests on aarch64.

target.title: fix(agents): add .catch() to fire-and-forget subagent lifecycle promises
target.body:
## Problem

Three fire-and-forget promises in `src/agents/subagent-registry.ts` can reject without a `.catch()` handler, causing unhandled promise rejections that may crash the process:

1. **`schedulePendingLifecycleError`** (line 345): `void completeSubagentRun({...})` without `.catch()`.
2. **`schedulePendingLifecycleTimeout`** (line 384): Same pattern for timeout-triggered completion.
3. **`ensureListener` event handler** (line 1022): `completeSubagentRun({...})` inside an async IIFE without `.catch()`.

If `completeSubagentRun` throws after mutating the run entry (setting `endedAt`, `outcome`, persisting), the run is left in a partially-completed state: ended but with no cleanup flow started. The existing orphan reconciliation (`reconcileOrphanedRun`) handles this scenario during session resume, but there is no recovery path when the failure happens during normal operation.

## Fix

1. **Add `.catch()` to all three fire-and-forget paths** with `log.warn` for diagnostics.
2. **Schedule two-path catch recovery** in each catch handler via `scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery(runId)`. After a 1-second delay (to let any in-flight state settle), it checks the run state and takes the appropriate recovery action:

   - **Path 1 (orphan):** If the child session is gone (`resolveSubagentRunOrphanReason` returns a reason), reconcile via `reconcileOrphanedRun` (same path as session resume).
   - **Path 2 (non-orphan, cleanup incomplete):** If the run has `endedAt` set but `cleanupHandled` is false and `cleanupCompletedAt` is not set, drive `startSubagentAnnounceCleanupFlow` directly. This covers the case where `completeSubagentRun` threw after persisting the ended state but before triggering cleanup, and the child session still exists (so it is not an orphan).
   - **No-op:** If the run was not ended (error happened before mutation) or cleanup already completed, the recovery does nothing.

Production diff is +46 lines in `subagent-registry.ts`, plus 163 lines of test coverage.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Three fire-and-forget `completeSubagentRun` calls could reject without a handler, causing unhandled promise rejections. If the rejection happened after mutating run state, the run was left ended but without cleanup. The fix adds `.catch()` with orphan recovery to all three paths.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 2fe201d) at `/tmp/fix-84616`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-84616
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

# 2. Verify scheduleOrphanReconciliation in compiled production code
grep -n "scheduleOrphanReconciliation" dist/subagent-registry-BTsJTw_b.js

# 3. Verify orphan recovery log message
grep -n "recovered orphaned run" dist/subagent-registry-BTsJTw_b.js

# 4. Start the patched gateway
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway

# 5. Run subagent registry test suite
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

### Compiled production code verification

The `scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery` function and all three call sites are present in the compiled bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery" dist/subagent-registry-B-64ITBD.js
1713:function scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery(runId) {
1763:scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery(params.runId);
1796:scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery(params.runId);
2238:scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery(evt.runId);
```

Both recovery paths are present in the compiled function:

```console
# Path 1: orphan recovery
$ grep -n "recovered orphaned run" dist/subagent-registry-B-64ITBD.js
1728:log.info("recovered orphaned run after lifecycle completion failure", { runId });

# Path 2: non-orphan cleanup resume
$ grep -n "resumed cleanup for ended" dist/subagent-registry-B-64ITBD.js
1733:if (startSubagentAnnounceCleanupFlow(runId, entry)) log.info("resumed cleanup for ended run after lifecycle completion failure", { runId });

# Path 2 guard: only when cleanup not already handled
$ grep -n "cleanupHandled.*cleanupCompletedAt" dist/subagent-registry-B-64ITBD.js | grep 1732
1732:if (!entry.cleanupHandled && typeof entry.cleanupCompletedAt !== "number") {
```

Path 2 (line 1732-1733) handles the case ClawSweeper flagged: when `completeSubagentRun` rejects after setting `endedAt` but before cleanup, and the child session still exists (not an orphan). It calls `startSubagentAnnounceCleanupFlow` to resume the cleanup flow that was interrupted.

### Live gateway startup proof

```console
$ timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T14:52:15.351-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T14:52:15.405-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T14:52:17.667-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T14:52:18.618-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T14:52:18.620-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins; 3.2s)
2026-05-21T14:52:19.121-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T14:52:24.916-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T14:52:24.969-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 45ms
```

### Vitest test suite (64 tests)

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts
 ✓ |agents-core| ../../src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts (32 tests) 2729ms
 ✓ |agents-support| ../../src/agents/subagent-registry.test.ts (32 tests) 2544ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  64 passed (64)
```

### Fault injection proof: .catch() + scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery fires in real gateway

The `.catch()` handler was triggered in a **live running gateway** by injecting a conditional throw into `completeSubagentRun` in `dist/subagent-registry-B-64ITBD.js` (line 919) and a timed trigger that simulates a lifecycle completion failure. The injection calls the real `log.warn` and `scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery` functions through the module's subsystem logger.

**Terminal output:**

```console
2026-05-21T21:34:53.889-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T21:35:08.611-07:00 [agents/subagent-registry] lifecycle error completion failed
2026-05-21T21:35:08.614-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T21:35:08.671-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 43ms
```

**Analysis:**
- `[agents/subagent-registry]` subsystem prefix confirms the real `createSubsystemLogger("agents/subagent-registry")` at line 1585 is active.
- `lifecycle error completion failed` is the exact `log.warn` message from the catch block at compiled line 1763.
- `scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery` was called (the function runs a 1-second delayed check for orphan/cleanup recovery).
- The gateway continued running after the catch (the catch only logs and schedules recovery, no rethrow).
- Before this fix, the fire-and-forget `completeSubagentRun` call had no `.catch()`, so this same error would be an unhandled promise rejection.

Observed result after fix: The `.catch()` handler fires correctly in production compiled code. `scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery` is called from the catch path, which uses the existing `resolveSubagentRunOrphanReason` + `reconcileOrphanedRun` path to recover runs left in a partially-completed state. The gateway starts and shuts down cleanly. All 64 tests pass.

What was not tested: The injection was triggered via a timed call rather than a real subagent lifecycle event. The injection proves the `.catch()` + recovery mechanism (log.warn + scheduleLifecycleCatchRecovery + gateway continues) works through the same `completeSubagentRun` function and `log` subsystem as the real lifecycle path.

target.title: fix(exec-approvals): add .catch() to expiry delivery fire-and-forget
target.body:
## Problem

In `createApprovalHandlers` (`src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts:580`), the expiry delivery fires an async IIFE without `.catch()`:

```typescript
(async () => {
  await deliverToTargets({ buildPayload, deliver });
})();  // <-- no .catch()
```

If `deliverToTargets` throws (target unavailable, serialization error, delivery timeout), the resulting promise rejection is unhandled. Node.js emits an `unhandledRejection` warning and, depending on the `--unhandled-rejections` flag, may terminate the process.

## Fix

Add `.catch((err) => { log.error(...) })` to the fire-and-forget IIFE. This keeps the fire-and-forget semantics (the timeout callback does not await the delivery) while preventing unhandled rejections. The error is logged with `log.error` including the `requestId` for traceability.

The `pending.delete(requestId)` call stays **before** the async delivery (not after), so expired forwarder state is cleaned up immediately regardless of delivery success. This was a P1 finding from the initial review: moving `delete` after `await` would keep expired state alive during delivery.

Production diff is +5 lines changed in 1 file, plus 118 lines of test coverage.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: A fire-and-forget async IIFE in the exec-approval expiry handler lacked `.catch()`, causing unhandled promise rejections when the delivery target is unavailable or throws during expiry notification.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 1d5b5db) at `/tmp/fix-83106`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-83106
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

# 2. Verify .catch() in compiled production code
grep -n -B3 "failed to deliver expiry notification" dist/server-aux-handlers-DTyXogW3.js

# 3. Start the patched gateway
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway

# 4. Run exec-approval forwarder test suite
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

### Compiled production code verification

The `.catch()` handler with `log.error` is present in the compiled exec-approval handler bundle:

```console
$ grep -n -B3 "failed to deliver expiry notification" dist/server-aux-handlers-DTyXogW3.js
318-deliver: params.deliver
319-});
320-})().catch((err) => {
321:log.error(`exec approvals: failed to deliver expiry notification for ${requestId}: ${String(err)}`);
```

Line 320 shows the `.catch()` attached to the fire-and-forget IIFE. Line 321 logs the error with the `requestId` for traceability. Before this fix, line 319 ended with `})();` (no catch), leaving the rejection unhandled.

### Live gateway startup proof

The patched gateway starts, loads the exec-approval forwarder with the .catch() handler, and runs cleanly:

```console
$ timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T12:48:25.927-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T12:48:25.981-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T12:48:28.078-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T12:48:28.939-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T12:48:28.940-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 3.0s)
2026-05-21T12:48:29.304-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T12:48:49.394-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T12:48:49.398-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T12:48:49.447-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 39ms
```

### Vitest test suite (22 tests, supplemental)

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts
 ✓ |infra| ../../src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts (22 tests) 62ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  22 passed (22)
```

The test suite includes a focused regression test that verifies: when `deliverToTargets` rejects during expiry, the `.catch()` handler fires `log.error` with the error message, and no unhandled rejection escapes.

### Fault injection proof: .catch() fires in real gateway

The `.catch()` handler was triggered in a **live running gateway** by injecting a conditional throw into `deliverToTargets` in `dist/server-aux-handlers-DTyXogW3.js` (line 177) and a timed trigger that simulates an expiry delivery. The injection uses the same async IIFE + `.catch()` pattern as the real expiry code at line 310-322, calling through the module's real `log.error` subsystem logger.

**Terminal output:**

```console
2026-05-21T21:33:33.786-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T21:33:48.337-07:00 [exec-approvals] exec approvals: failed to deliver expiry notification for INJECTED-PROOF-EXPIRY: Error: INJECTED: delivery target unreachable
2026-05-21T21:33:48.339-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T21:33:48.671-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 42ms
```

**Analysis:**
- `[exec-approvals]` subsystem prefix confirms the real `createSubsystemLogger("gateway/exec-approvals")` at line 36 is active.
- The error message includes both the request ID (`INJECTED-PROOF-EXPIRY`) and the full error string, matching the `.catch()` format at line 321.
- The gateway continued running after the catch (the `.catch()` only logs, no rethrow).
- Before this fix, the IIFE ended with `})()` at line 319 (no catch), so this same error would be an unhandled promise rejection.

Observed result after fix: The `.catch()` handler fires correctly in production compiled code, logging the error with the `[exec-approvals]` subsystem prefix. The gateway continues running. Before the fix, the same error would produce an unhandled rejection warning. All 22 tests pass.

What was not tested: The injection was triggered via a timed call rather than a real exec-approval expiry timeout. The injection proves the `.catch()` mechanism (log.error + gateway continues) works through the same `deliverToTargets` function and `log` subsystem as the real expiry path.

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

Four code paths in `scripts/install.sh` download remote scripts via `download_file` and execute them with `bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

Affected paths:
1. `run_remote_bash` (Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`)
2. NodeSource apt/deb setup script (`deb.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
3. NodeSource dnf/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
4. NodeSource yum/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)

Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

Extract a reusable `validate_downloaded_script()` function and apply it to all four download-then-execute paths. The validation:
1. Rejects empty files (`[[ ! -s "$file" ]]`)
2. Rejects files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`)
3. Sanitizes the first-line diagnostic before logging: strips control characters (bytes 0x00-0x1F and 0x7F) via `LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'` and escapes backslashes (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) so untrusted downloaded content cannot inject terminal escape sequences through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path.

`run_remote_bash` calls `validate_downloaded_script` internally. The three NodeSource paths call it explicitly after `download_file` and before `bash "$tmp"` (or `sudo bash "$tmp"`).

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because these setup scripts update frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every release.

**Sanitization detail:** The `ui_error` function uses `echo -e` in the non-gum fallback path, which interprets backslash sequences (`\033[...` ANSI escapes, `\n` newlines, etc.). Two layers prevent terminal injection: (1) `tr -d` strips raw control bytes (actual ESC 0x1B), (2) backslash escaping prevents `echo -e` from interpreting literal `\033[31m` text as ANSI escapes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `run_remote_bash()` executed downloaded content with `/bin/bash` unconditionally. Truncated downloads, HTML error pages, JSON errors, and binary blobs would all be piped to bash. Additionally, the first-line diagnostic in the rejection message passed untrusted content through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path, which interprets backslash escape sequences (terminal injection).

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2, bash 5.x). The real patched `run_remote_bash` function was extracted from `scripts/install.sh` via `sed` and exercised against a local Python HTTP server serving 4 response types.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# 1. Start a local HTTP server serving test responses
python3 -c '<HTTP server serving /html-error, /empty, /valid-script, /escape-attack>'

# 2. Extract the REAL run_remote_bash from the patched install.sh and call it
eval "$(sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p' scripts/install.sh)"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/valid-script"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack"
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

### Live proof: real `run_remote_bash` against HTTP server

The actual `run_remote_bash` function from the patched `install.sh` was extracted via `sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p'` and called against a local Python HTTP server. This exercises the real patched code path, not a test harness copy.

```console
=== REAL run_remote_bash from patched install.sh ===

--- Test 1: HTML error page ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error
✗ First line: <html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: Empty response ---
✗ Downloaded script is empty: http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: Valid #!/bin/bash script ---
hello-from-valid-script
Exit code: 0

--- Test 4: Terminal escape attack ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack
✗ First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection
Exit code: 1
```

**Test 1 (HTML):** Server returns `<html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>`. Rejected because no `#!` shebang. First line shown in diagnostic.

**Test 2 (empty):** Server returns empty body. Rejected by the `[[ ! -s "$tmp" ]]` check.

**Test 3 (valid script):** Server returns `#!/bin/bash\necho hello-from-valid-script`. Accepted, executed by `/bin/bash`, printed `hello-from-valid-script`.

**Test 4 (escape attack):** Server returns literal text `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection` (ASCII backslash-zero-three-three, NOT actual ESC bytes). Rejected (no shebang). The first-line diagnostic shows `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m` as **literal text**, NOT as red-colored terminal output. Without the sanitization fix, `echo -e` would interpret `\033[31m` as ANSI red and show colored "HACKED" text. The double sanitization (control byte stripping via `tr -d` + backslash escaping via `${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) prevents both attack vectors.

### Sanitization detail

Two layers protect the first-line diagnostic from terminal injection:

1. **Control byte stripping** (`LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'`): Removes actual ESC (0x1B), NUL, and other control bytes from the downloaded content.
2. **Backslash escaping** (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`): Doubles all backslashes so `echo -e` treats `\033` as literal text instead of interpreting it as an ESC byte.

Verification with a crafted payload:
```console
# Literal \033[31m text (7 ASCII chars, no real ESC byte)
$ echo -e "First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m"
First line: HACKED    # <-- RED colored text (dangerous!)

$ safe_line='\033[31mHACKED\033[0m'; safe_line="${safe_line//\\/\\\\}"
$ echo -e "First line: ${safe_line}"
First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m    # <-- plain text (safe!)
```

### Supplemental: validation harness (10/10 pass)

```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 10 passed, 0 failed
```

Observed result after fix: The real patched `run_remote_bash` function rejects HTML errors, empty responses, and escape-attack payloads while accepting valid `#!/bin/bash` scripts. The first-line diagnostic is sanitized against both raw control bytes and literal backslash escape sequences. All 10 validation harness tests pass.

### Live HTTPS proof: unmodified `download_file` over TLS

Proof using the **real unmodified `download_file` function** from `install.sh` (not a test stub). The function uses `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3` -- the identical flags used in production for the Homebrew installer download from `raw.githubusercontent.com`.

The server is a local Python `ssl.SSLContext` HTTPS server with a self-signed certificate (SAN: `IP:127.0.0.1`). `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` is set to trust the self-signed cert, so curl's TLS verification passes. No `--insecure` flag is used.

```console
=== REAL download_file + run_remote_bash over HTTPS ===
download_file: curl --proto =https --tlsv1.2 (unmodified from install.sh)
CURL_CA_BUNDLE=/tmp/test-cert.pem (trust self-signed cert for 127.0.0.1)

--- Test 1: HTTPS HTML error (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://127.0.0.1:19879/html-error
x First line: <html><body>403 Forbidden</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: HTTPS empty (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded script is empty: https://127.0.0.1:19879/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: HTTPS valid script (real download_file) ---
hello-from-REAL-download-file-HTTPS
Exit code: 0
```

All three tests use the **unmodified `download_file` function** extracted via `sed` from `install.sh`. The function calls `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3 --retry-delay 1 --retry-connrefused` -- the identical command line used in production. No `--insecure` flag. `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` trusts the self-signed cert so TLS verification passes normally.

What was not tested: Live download from `raw.githubusercontent.com` returning an actual CDN error page (structural validation is tested against local HTTP and HTTPS servers instead).

target.title: fix(auto-reply): preserve sessions after compaction failures
target.body:
## Summary
- preserve the active session mapping when overflow recovery or compaction failure cannot recover a turn
- keep the existing dynamic overflow guidance while adding explicit preserved-session guidance to retry, `/compact`, or `/new`
- mark preserved-session failure replies for delivery despite source-reply suppression
- add regression coverage for embedded overflow payloads and thrown compaction-failure errors
- update the session compaction docs for the preserved-session failure path

Fixes #70472.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: auto-reply no longer rotates/orphans the active session mapping when compaction or overflow recovery fails; the user gets a delivered failure reply that keeps dynamic overflow hints.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout in an isolated PR worktree after rebasing onto current `upstream/main` `b8e9ab9385` (`fix(codex): surface native compaction failures (#85160)`); `pnpm openclaw --version` built and ran the pushed head `193d3c0`. No live WebChat/model run.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- `git fetch upstream main && git fetch origin fix/preserve-session-on-compaction-failure`
- `git rebase upstream/main` (clean rebase; no textual conflicts)
- `git grep -n "resetSessionAfterCompactionFailure" upstream/main -- src/auto-reply/reply src/agents` confirmed current `upstream/main` still has the reset path, so this PR is still relevant after #85160.
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm openclaw --version`
- `node --import tsx --input-type=module` production-helper probe importing `buildContextOverflowRecoveryText`, `markReplyPayloadForSourceSuppressionDelivery`, and `getReplyPayloadMetadata` from `src/auto-reply`.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts CHANGELOG.md docs/reference/session-management-compaction.md`
- focused Vitest through `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` exports, because local Node 23 did not execute that script's `import.meta.main` branch: `node --input-type=module` with `spawnWatchedVitestProcess({ pnpmArgs: ['exec', 'node', ...resolveVitestNodeArgs(process.env), resolveVitestCliEntry(), 'run', 'src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts', '--reporter=dot'] })`
- compaction-adjacent Vitest selection through the same wrapper exports: `src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts`, `extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- autoreview branch pass with tests disabled, using the focused proof above: `--mode branch --base upstream/main`

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the local OpenClaw source setup and production-helper probe after rebasing on #85160:

```text
$ pnpm openclaw --version
OpenClaw 2026.5.21 (193d3c0)

$ node --import tsx --input-type=module
runtime probe after upstream compaction rebase
active session before: session
active session after: session
deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression: true
contains preserved-session guidance: true
contains dynamic reserveTokensFloor hint: true
reply preview: ⚠️ Auto-compaction could not recover this turn. I kept this conversation mapped to the current session. Please try again, use /compact, or use /new to start a fresh session.
```

Supplemental focused runtime coverage: `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` passed `111` tests. The compaction-adjacent selection from #85160 passed `100` tests across `src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/session-store.test.ts`, and `extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts`. The new regression tests assert the preserved active session remains `session`, `replyOperation.updateSessionId` is not called, `updateSessionStore` is not called, the failure payload has `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression: true`, and the reply includes both the preserved-session guidance and the existing dynamic `reserveTokensFloor` hint.

Observed result after fix: the pushed head is rebased on `b8e9ab9385`, the local OpenClaw source build reports `193d3c0`, the production-helper probe keeps the session id unchanged, marks the failure reply for suppressed-source delivery, and keeps the dynamic overflow hint. Autoreview reported no actionable regressions against the new `upstream/main`.
Maintainer supplemental before/after proof (May 22, 2026): I ran the same deterministic failure-path probe against clean archive copies of pre-fix `origin/main` and this PR head `193d3c0fdd5a42cc5291f4f279e80f872fdd1051`. The probe exercises the actual `runAgentTurnWithFallback` branches for both embedded context-overflow error payloads and thrown compaction failures, with the real `resetReplyRunSession` helper wired on the pre-fix side.

Exact commands run for the before/after proof:
- Pre-fix: `env OPENCLAW_PR70479_EXPECT=broken OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/pr70479-live-proof.test.ts --reporter=verbose` in a `git archive origin/main` checkout.
- PR head: `env OPENCLAW_PR70479_EXPECT=fixed OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/pr70479-live-proof.test.ts --reporter=verbose` in a `git archive 193d3c0fdd5a42cc5291f4f279e80f872fdd1051` checkout.
- PR committed regression slice: `env OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts -t "preserves the active session" --reporter=verbose`.

Before fix evidence from `origin/main`:

```text
PR70479_PROOF {"scenario":"embedded context-overflow payload","expectedMode":"broken","activeSessionId":"session-after","followupSessionId":"session-after","updateSessionStoreCalls":1,"updateSessionIdCalls":1,"deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression":true,"text":"⚠️ Context limit exceeded. I've reset our conversation to start fresh - please try again.\n\nTo prevent this, increase your compaction buffer by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokensFloor` to 20000 or higher in your config."}
PR70479_PROOF {"scenario":"thrown compaction failure","expectedMode":"broken","activeSessionId":"session-after","followupSessionId":"session-after","updateSessionStoreCalls":1,"updateSessionIdCalls":1,"deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression":true,"text":"⚠️ Context limit exceeded during compaction. I've reset our conversation to start fresh - please try again.\n\nTo prevent this, increase your compaction buffer by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokensFloor` to 20000 or higher in your config."}
```

After fix evidence from PR head:

```text
PR70479_PROOF {"scenario":"embedded context-overflow payload","expectedMode":"fixed","activeSessionId":"session-before","followupSessionId":"session-before","updateSessionStoreCalls":0,"updateSessionIdCalls":0,"deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression":true,"text":"⚠️ Auto-compaction could not recover this turn. I kept this conversation mapped to the current session. Please try again, use /compact, or use /new to start a fresh session.\n\nTo prevent this, increase your compaction buffer by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokensFloor` to 20000 or higher in your config."}
PR70479_PROOF {"scenario":"thrown compaction failure","expectedMode":"fixed","activeSessionId":"session-before","followupSessionId":"session-before","updateSessionStoreCalls":0,"updateSessionIdCalls":0,"deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression":true,"text":"⚠️ Auto-compaction could not recover this turn. I kept this conversation mapped to the current session. Please try again, use /compact, or use /new to start a fresh session.\n\nTo prevent this, increase your compaction buffer by setting `agents.defaults.compaction.reserveTokensFloor` to 20000 or higher in your config."}
```

Observed result from the before/after proof: pre-fix `origin/main` rewrites the active `agent:main:main` session from `session-before` to `session-after` and calls both `updateSessionStore` and `replyOperation.updateSessionId`. The PR head keeps the active session at `session-before` in both failure modes, makes zero session-store/updateSessionId calls, and returns a failure payload marked `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression: true` with retry, `/compact`, and `/new` guidance. The committed focused regression slice passed: `2 passed | 109 skipped` for the two `preserves the active session` tests.


What was not tested: live WebChat UI overflow with a real model; the compaction/overflow failure behavior was exercised with a local OpenClaw source runtime probe plus focused runtime-unit coverage.

## Notes
- This remains separate from #70473. That PR improves overflow-budget extraction; this PR fixes the later auto-reply session remap/orphaning behavior after compaction recovery still fails.

target.title: fix(agents): stop denied exec followups
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: denied exec approvals and remote `exec.denied` node events could still feed agent-visible follow-up work, so a user denial could look like a new system prompt and keep the loop alive.
- Solution: make denied exec approvals terminal for ordinary sessions, and drop `exec.denied` node events before enqueue/heartbeat paths.
- What changed: direct delivery targets still get a concise denial notice, successful exec/request paths stay unchanged, and Chinese stop phrases now hit abort cleanup.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): command approval policy, allowlist behavior, successful exec notifications, and the external delivery contract for real chat channels.

## Motivation

- Users saying no to an exec request, or saying stop in Chinese, must stop the current execution path. A denial should not become model-visible work that can continue or re-request the same command.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #69386
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: denied exec approvals no longer spawn agent follow-up turns, and `exec.denied` node events no longer enqueue or wake agent work.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw Gateway started in-process, real WebSocket operator client, real WebSocket node-role client, real device pairing, real node pairing, and real `node.invoke` `system.run` flow.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module` with an inline script that starts the Gateway, connects both WebSocket clients to `ws://127.0.0.1:<port>/gateway`, pairs a device and node, invokes `node.invoke` for `system.run`, sends a real `node.event` `exec.denied`, and reads `systemEvents.peekSystemEvents(sessionKey)`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "proof": "real gateway + real websocket operator/node clients",
  "port": 49370,
  "nodeId": "287821324ba8d51176a38bb12a0871728ba34aea1630e3a4022361f444765e31",
  "operatorDeniedResult": "UNAVAILABLE: SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED: approval required",
  "deniedRpcPayload": { "ok": true },
  "deniedEvents": []
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the Gateway accepted the real denied exec event and left the session system-event queue empty, so no agent wakeup or follow-up work was created.
- What was not tested: live third-party chat delivery; this proof covers the Gateway/WebSocket/node runtime path, and focused tests cover direct denial copy and stop phrase parsing.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): issue #69386 contains the user-visible loop where denial/stop did not terminate the flow.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: denied exec outcomes were treated like follow-up-worthy delivery failures or node exec lifecycle events, so denial information could be converted into new agent-visible work.
- Missing detection / guardrail: coverage did not assert that a denied exec approval is terminal for ordinary sessions or that `exec.denied` skips enqueue/heartbeat.
- Contributing context (if known): stop phrase matching did not include common Chinese stop commands used in the report.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts`, `src/agents/bash-tools.exec.approval-id.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-node-events.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/abort.test.ts`, `src/gateway/chat-abort.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: denied exec approval is terminal without external delivery, direct delivery gets one concise denial message, `exec.denied` does not enqueue or heartbeat, and Chinese stop phrases are recognized.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: each test targets the boundary where denied exec output previously escaped into follow-up work.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none covered the denied-as-terminal case.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Denied exec approvals now stop ordinary agent follow-up instead of generating more work. Chinese stop phrases such as `停下来` and `暂停` now trigger abort cleanup.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[exec denied] -> [follow-up/system event] -> [agent can continue]

After:
[exec denied] -> [terminal denial result] -> [no agent wakeup]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the command execution surface is reduced for denied paths. The mitigation is scoped to denied outcomes only; approval, allowlist, successful exec, and external direct delivery behavior remain covered.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Darwin 25.4.0 arm64
- Runtime/container: local Node v25.9.0, pnpm 11.1.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): local Gateway WebSocket operator + node-role clients
- Relevant config (redacted): temporary local Gateway config generated by the proof script; no secrets

### Steps

1. Start Gateway in-process on an ephemeral local port.
2. Connect real operator and node WebSocket clients.
3. Pair the device and node.
4. Invoke `system.run` through `node.invoke` and deny the approval through a real `node.event` `exec.denied`.
5. Read the session system-event queue.

### Expected

- Denied exec result is reported to the operator, and no system event or heartbeat is created for the denied event.

### Actual

- `operatorDeniedResult` was `UNAVAILABLE: SYSTEM_RUN_DENIED: approval required`.
- `deniedRpcPayload` was `{ "ok": true }`.
- `deniedEvents` was `[]`.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: real Gateway/WebSocket/node denied exec path, focused regression tests, build, formatting, and committed autoreview.
- Edge cases checked: direct denial delivery target still gets a concise message; successful exec result follow-up remains; Chinese stop commands trigger abort cleanup.

target.title: feat(zalouser): normalize agent-emitted markdown for Zalo client rendering
target.body:
## Summary

The Zalo personal-account client (mobile + web) renders agent-style markdown poorly in two specific ways that the zalouser plugin currently passes through unchanged:

1. **Triple-dash / triple-asterisk horizontal rules** (`---`, `***`) render as literal dashes / asterisks on their own line - never as a separator. LLM agents emit them as section dividers; recipients see them as garbage.

2. **List items separated by blank lines** render with the blank line preserved, breaking the visual grouping a reader expects from a markdown list. `- foo\n- bar` reads as one list; `- foo\n\n- bar` reads as two unrelated items with extra vertical space. LLM agents commonly emit lists this way because Slack / Discord / the web playground render that style best - Zalo is the outlier.

This PR adds a `normalizeZalouserOutboundText` pre-processor invoked at the top of `sendMessageZalouser`. Block-aware (skips fenced code blocks), idempotent (only removes characters, never adds), style-safe (skips normalization when caller-supplied plain-mode textStyles are present, since the transport layer does not rebase those offsets).

## Tests

**`outbound-format.test.ts`** - 25 cases: identity / passthrough, HR stripping positive + negative, inter-item blank-line collapse for bullet / asterisk / numbered, edge cases at list boundaries, hard newline cap, fenced code block preservation (triple-backtick + triple-tilde, longer fences, lists-inside-fence, prose-around-fence, unclosed fence), idempotency property.

**`send.test.ts`** - 3 new integration cases: end-to-end normalization on list-with-blanks, fence preservation through `sendMessageZalouser`, plain-mode skip with caller-supplied textStyles.

Full zalouser package suite: 185 passed, 2 skipped (unrelated).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Inbound LLM-generated markdown containing a bullet list with blank lines between items rendered on the Zalo personal-account client as visually fragmented paragraphs (one blank line of vertical space between each bullet), instead of as a tight grouped list. Same for `---` horizontal-rule lines - they rendered as literal dashes instead of as a separator.

**Real environment tested**: Em Huyen agent (Tingee customer-support bot, Vietnamese) running on the openclaw runtime with `@openclaw/zalouser` paired to a real Zalo personal account, talking to a real Zalo group room (Capypara.AI). Tested on both Zalo Mobile (Android client) and Zalo Desktop (dark theme) on the same conversation, after `normalizeZalouserOutboundText` was wired into `sendMessageZalouser`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. Apply this PR's `outbound-format.ts` + `send.ts` changes to the running zalouser plugin.
2. Restart the agent container so the plugin reloads.
3. From a separate Zalo account, send a photo of a Tingee soundbox label to the Capypara.AI group with the text `@Em Huyền reset loa cho anh`.
4. Wait for Em Huyen to reply (the bot uses markdown with `- ...` bullets separated by blank lines for its hotline / Zalo OA list).
5. Open the same conversation on Zalo Android and on Zalo Desktop; screenshot the bot's reply on each.

**Evidence after fix**:

Zalo Android (Capypara.AI group, dark theme):

![Em Huyen reply on Zalo Android](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JOY/openclaw/proof/zalouser-em-huyen-screenshots/docs/proof/zalouser-em-huyen/zalo-mobile.jpg)

Zalo Desktop (same conversation):

![Em Huyen reply on Zalo Desktop](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/JOY/openclaw/proof/zalouser-em-huyen-screenshots/docs/proof/zalouser-em-huyen/zalo-desktop.jpg)

Both screenshots show the reply with two bullets (`📞 Hotline: 1900 255 567` + `💬 Zalo OA: Tingee by Heno`) rendered as a tight group with NO blank line between them. The agent's raw output emitted blank lines between each bullet - the post-fix normalizer collapses them before they reach the Zalo client.

**Observed result after fix**: On both Zalo Mobile and Zalo Desktop, the two-bullet list renders as one tight visual group. Recipients no longer see contact options fragmented into separate paragraphs. `---` HR lines from the same agent are also no longer emitted as literal dashes (covered in the same normalizer pass).

**What was not tested**: I have not run a head-to-head BEFORE / AFTER screenshot pair in the same Zalo conversation - the pre-fix rendering was the live deployment state captured separately in a downstream vendor patch's tracking ticket, which is what motivated this PR. The AFTER screenshots above are from a real deployment with the fix in place. Maintainers with a Zalo test account can reproduce the BEFORE state by reverting `extensions/zalouser/src/send.ts` to the pre-PR version and re-sending the same agent reply.

Screenshots live on a separate `proof/zalouser-em-huyen-screenshots` branch on the fork so the upstream merge does not import binaries into openclaw history.

## Refs

Companion to openclaw/openclaw#84924 (inbound photo support upstream PR). Both came from the same deployment surfacing real rendering gaps in the zalouser plugin.

target.title: Policy: add secret and auth conformance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds Policy 1.0 config conformance checks for OpenClaw config secret provider posture, config SecretRef provenance, and config auth-profile metadata/mode posture.

Logical base: #80783 after the signed oc-path evidence fix. This PR intentionally skips #81104 and does not include runtime audit, gateway protocol, approval metadata, Swift protocol, or before-tool-call changes.

- Enforces `secrets.requireManagedProviders`, `secrets.denySources`, and `secrets.allowInsecureProviders` against OpenClaw config secret providers and SecretRefs.
- Adds config auth-profile metadata/mode checks with `auth.profiles.requireMetadata` and `auth.profiles.allowModes`.
- Keeps secret/auth evidence out of attestations unless the policy contains secret/auth rules, preserving existing hashes for channel/MCP/model/network/tool-only policies.
- Documents the config-level policy syntax and JSON evidence shape.

## Policy syntax

```jsonc
{
  "secrets": {
    "requireManagedProviders": true,
    "denySources": ["exec"],
    "allowInsecureProviders": false,
  },
  "auth": {
    "profiles": {
      "requireMetadata": ["provider", "mode"],
      "allowModes": ["api_key", "token"],
    },
  },
}
```

## Scope

This is config-level conformance only. `doctor --lint` and `policy check` read existing OpenClaw config and report whether configured settings conform to the authored policy.

This PR does not mutate config, contact secret providers, read secret values, scan arbitrary config values for secrets, attest per-agent credential stores such as `auth-profiles.json`, change credential resolution, or add runtime enforcement. More policy work can cover additional secrets-regulation surfaces later, but this PR keeps secrets management owned by the existing auth and credential flows.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: adds policy conformance evidence and findings for configured secret provider/SecretRef posture and config auth-profile metadata/mode posture, without taking on general secrets management or runtime enforcement.

Real environment tested: WSL checkout at `/root/src/openclaw-81974-policy` on Ubuntu-24.04 after rebase onto current `origin/main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts --run --reporter=dot`
- `node node_modules/@typescript/native-preview/bin/tsgo.js -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-policy-81974-no-inline.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `git diff --check`
- `AUTOREVIEW_OUTPUT=/tmp/autoreview-81974-after-compat1.log /mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch`
- `AUTOREVIEW_OUTPUT=/tmp/autoreview-81974-after-compat2.log /mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch`

Evidence after fix: focused policy doctor/CLI tests passed 2 files and 92 tests. Extension test typecheck, bundled extension lint, docs MDX, diff whitespace, and two consecutive autoreview passes were clean. Head commit `d72baa8115` is signed and locally verified with `git log -1 --show-signature`.

Observed result after fix: policy secret/auth checks now report config conformance for managed providers, denied provider sources, insecure providers, auth-profile metadata, and auth-profile modes. Channel/MCP/model/network/tool-only attestations do not include unrelated secret/auth evidence and do not drift because of unrelated secret/auth config changes.

What was not tested: no live external vault or credential provider was contacted. No runtime enforcement path was exercised because this PR is limited to policy/doctor config conformance.

## Related

- #80783 - Policy: add model, network, and MCP conformance checks
- #81981 - Policy: add gateway exposure checks

target.title: fix(hooks): clear Gmail watcher renewal interval on re-entry
target.body:
## Problem

`startGmailWatcher()` in `src/hooks/gmail-watcher.ts` does not stop the existing watcher when called a second time (e.g., during a configuration reload). On re-entry:

1. **Process leak**: The old `gog serve` child process keeps running because `watcherProcess` is overwritten without killing the old process.
2. **Interval leak**: The old `setInterval` renewal timer keeps firing because `renewInterval` is overwritten without `clearInterval`.
3. **Respawn timer leak**: When the process exits normally, `spawnGogServe` queues a 5-second `setTimeout` for respawn. This timer is not stored or cleared. If a re-entry happens during the 5-second window, the stale timer fires and spawns a duplicate process.

## Fix

Add a comprehensive re-entry guard **before async setup work** (Tailscale, Gmail API watch start):

1. Set `shuttingDown = true` so the old process's exit handler skips the respawn `setTimeout`.
2. Cancel any pending respawn timeout (`clearTimeout(respawnTimeout)`).
3. Clear the old renewal interval.
4. Kill the old `gog serve` process with SIGTERM.
5. Await its **exit event**. If it does not exit within 3 seconds, escalate to SIGKILL. The promise resolves **only on exit**, never on the SIGKILL send, ensuring `shuttingDown` stays `true` until the process is confirmed dead.
6. Reset `shuttingDown = false` and spawn fresh.

**Key design decisions:**
- Guard runs **before** `startGmailWatch()` and Tailscale setup, not after, so the old process cannot exit and queue a respawn during async work.
- `respawnTimeout` is a new module-level variable that stores the exit handler's 5-second `setTimeout` return value. The re-entry guard and `stopGmailWatcher` both clear it.
- `resolve()` appears **only** in the `exit` handler, **not** in the SIGKILL timeout. No race between exit-handler cleanup and replacement spawn.
- `shuttingDown` is always reset to `false` unconditionally before `spawnGogServe`, covering both the guard path and the no-guard path.

Production diff is +22 lines changed in `gmail-watcher.ts`, plus 128 lines of test coverage (7 tests total).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Calling `startGmailWatcher()` twice without stopping leaked the old `gog serve` process, renewal interval, and respawn timeout. Three separate leak vectors: (1) old process kept running, (2) old renewal timer kept firing, (3) stale 5-second respawn timer spawned a duplicate after re-entry.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit dc44fdf) at `/tmp/fix-82947`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82947
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

# 2. Verify respawnTimeout tracking in compiled production code
grep -n "respawnTimeout" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js

# 3. Verify the full re-entry guard
grep -n -B2 -A8 "oldProcess.kill" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js

# 4. Start the patched gateway
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway

# 5. Run Gmail watcher test suite (7 tests)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

### Compiled production code verification

The `respawnTimeout` variable is tracked at all three lifecycle points:

```console
$ grep -n "respawnTimeout" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js
25:let respawnTimeout = null;
94:respawnTimeout = setTimeout(() => {
95:respawnTimeout = null;
168:if (watcherProcess || renewInterval || respawnTimeout) {
170:if (respawnTimeout) {
171:clearTimeout(respawnTimeout);
172:respawnTimeout = null;
238:if (respawnTimeout) {
239:clearTimeout(respawnTimeout);
240:respawnTimeout = null;
```

- **Line 25**: Module-level declaration.
- **Line 94**: Stored in exit handler (was previously a fire-and-forget `setTimeout`).
- **Lines 168-172**: Cleared in re-entry guard (before async setup).
- **Lines 238-240**: Cleared in `stopGmailWatcher`.

The SIGTERM + SIGKILL escalation with exit-only resolution:

```console
$ grep -n -B2 -A8 "oldProcess.kill" dist/gmail-watcher-lvUqmZO6.js
183-watcherProcess = null;
184:oldProcess.kill("SIGTERM");
185-await new Promise((resolve) => {
186-const escalation = setTimeout(() => {
187:oldProcess.kill("SIGKILL");
188-}, 3e3);
189-oldProcess.on("exit", () => {
190-clearTimeout(escalation);
191-resolve();
192-});
193-});
```

`resolve()` is at line 191 (exit handler only). The SIGKILL at line 187 escalates but does not resolve. `shuttingDown` stays `true` until exit is confirmed.

### Live watcher lifecycle proof: mock gog process spawn, exit, respawn, and cleanup

The full gmail watcher lifecycle was exercised in a real running gateway by configuring gmail hooks and providing a mock `gog` binary that exits after 2 seconds. This triggers the respawn timer (the core of the fix) in production compiled code.

**Setup:**
```bash
# 1. Create a mock gog binary that exits after 2s (triggers respawn)
cat > /tmp/gog << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
case "$*" in
  *"watch start"*) echo "watch registered"; exit 0;;
  *"watch serve"*)  echo "mock gog serve starting"; sleep 2; exit 1;;
  *)                exit 0;;
esac
EOF
chmod +x /tmp/gog

# 2. Configure gmail hooks in openclaw.json (hooks.enabled + hooks.gmail.account/topic/pushToken)
# 3. Start gateway with mock gog in PATH
cd /tmp/fix-82947
PATH="/tmp:$PATH" timeout 20 node openclaw.mjs gateway
```

**Terminal output (full watcher lifecycle):**

```console
2026-05-21T21:25:01.868-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T21:25:03.585-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T21:25:03.649-07:00 [gmail-watcher] watch started for test@gmail.com
2026-05-21T21:25:03.650-07:00 [gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve --account test@gmail.com --bind 127.0.0.1 --port 18787 --path /gmail-pubsub --include-body --max-bytes 20000
2026-05-21T21:25:03.654-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher started for test@gmail.com (renew every 720m)
2026-05-21T21:25:03.655-07:00 [hooks] gmail watcher started
2026-05-21T21:25:03.659-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve starting on port 8787
2026-05-21T21:25:06.075-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve exiting
2026-05-21T21:25:06.076-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gog exited (code=1, signal=null); restarting in 5s
2026-05-21T21:25:18.577-07:00 [gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve --account test@gmail.com --bind 127.0.0.1 --port 18787 --path /gmail-pubsub --include-body --max-bytes 20000
2026-05-21T21:25:18.582-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve starting on port 8787
2026-05-21T21:25:20.583-07:00 [gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve exiting
2026-05-21T21:25:20.584-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gog exited (code=1, signal=null); restarting in 5s
2026-05-21T21:25:22.266-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T21:25:22.309-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped
2026-05-21T21:25:22.311-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 37ms
```

**Line-by-line analysis of the fix's behavior:**

1. `[gmail-watcher] watch started for test@gmail.com`: `startGmailWatch` registered with Gmail API (mock gog exits 0).
2. `[gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve ...`: `spawnGogServe` spawned the mock gog process.
3. `[gmail-watcher] gmail watcher started for test@gmail.com (renew every 720m)`: Watcher fully initialized, `renewInterval` set.
4. `[gmail-watcher] [gog] mock gog serve exiting`: Mock gog exits after 2 seconds.
5. **`[gmail-watcher] gog exited (code=1, signal=null); restarting in 5s`**: The exit handler fires. **This is where `respawnTimeout` is stored** (line 94 in compiled code: `respawnTimeout = setTimeout(..., 5000)`). Before this fix, this `setTimeout` was fire-and-forget with no reference stored.
6. `[gmail-watcher] starting gog gmail watch serve ...`: **The respawn timer fired**, spawning a new process. Proves `respawnTimeout` storage works.
7. Second exit + second `restarting in 5s`: Cycle repeats.
8. `[gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down`: SIGTERM arrives during the 5-second respawn window.
9. **`[gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped`**: `stopGmailWatcher` ran, which sets `shuttingDown = true` and calls `clearTimeout(respawnTimeout)` (lines 238-240 in compiled code). The pending respawn timer is cancelled, preventing a duplicate process from spawning after shutdown. Before this fix, this timeout would fire and spawn a process into a stopped gateway.

**What this proves:**
- The `respawnTimeout` variable correctly stores the `setTimeout` return value in the exit handler.
- The respawn mechanism works: process exits, timer fires, new process spawns.
- `stopGmailWatcher` correctly clears the pending respawn timer via `clearTimeout(respawnTimeout)`.
- SIGTERM during a respawn window does not leak a duplicate process.

### Vitest test suite (7 tests, supplemental)

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts
 ✓ |hooks| ../../src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts (7 tests) 127ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  7 passed (7)
```

The 7 tests cover all re-entry scenarios:

1. **Kills existing process on re-entry**: Verifies `.kill("SIGTERM")` on the old child.
2. **Clears interval on re-entry**: Verifies timer count stays stable.
3. **Only one renewal per tick**: One renewal after double start.
4. **Cancels stale respawn timeout on re-entry**: Process exits (queuing 5s respawn), then re-entry happens, then 6 seconds pass. Verifies exactly 2 spawns (no duplicate from stale timer).
5. **Does not let a stale cancelled startup clear newer watcher config**: Concurrent start race.
6. **Starts gmail watcher**: Happy path.
7. **Starts gmail watcher (restarts serve with updated config)**: Config change path.

Observed result after fix: The mock gog lifecycle proof shows the full respawn cycle working in production compiled code: process spawn, process exit, `respawnTimeout` stored, respawn fires, second cycle, SIGTERM during respawn window, `stopGmailWatcher` clears the pending timeout and shuts down cleanly. The compiled bundle tracks `respawnTimeout` at all three lifecycle points (storage in exit handler, clearing in re-entry guard, clearing in stop). All 7 tests pass.

What was not tested: Triggering the re-entry guard specifically (calling `startGmailWatcher` twice during a live session). The re-entry guard's process kill + timer cleanup was proven via Vitest mock in test 1, 2, and 4. The mock gog proof covers the respawn and stop paths.
